title: Erdős's Problem on Vertex-Critical Graphs Far from Edge-Criticality
statement_text: Is it true that for every $k \geq 4$ and $r$ there is a vertex-critical $k$-chromatic graph which remains $k$-chromatic if any $r$ of its edges are omitted?
context_text: Erdős posed this problem in 1985 as a wide-ranging strengthening of Dirac's conjecture, asking whether $k$-chromatic vertex-critical graphs can be made arbitrarily far (in terms of edges removed) from any $(k-1)$-chromatic spanning subgraph. It is listed as Problem 5.14 in Jensen–Toft and appears in the Chung–Graham Erdős problem collection.
paper_contribution: Proves the statement for every fixed $r \in \mathbb{N}$ and all sufficiently large $k \geq k_0(r)$ (Theorem 1), via a probabilistic construction of uniform hypergraphs whose 2-section complements yield the required vertex-critical graphs.
notes: The paper's abstract explicitly states it 'partially solves a problem posed by Erdős in 1985'. The verbatim quote from Erdős (1985, p. 113) is reproduced in the introduction.

title: Open case of Erdős's problem for fixed small k
statement_text: For every fixed $k \geq 4$ and arbitrarily large $r$, does there exist a $k$-chromatic vertex-critical graph $G$ such that $\chi(G - R) = k$ for every $R \subseteq E(G)$ with $|R| \leq r$?
context_text: Theorem 1 resolves Erdős's problem for all sufficiently large $k$ (as a function of $r$), but leaves open the regime where $k \geq 4$ is held fixed as a small constant while $r$ grows. The authors explicitly flag this as an interesting remaining open case.
notes: Stated in prose without a labelled environment: 'Our result still leaves open Erdős' question when k ≥ 4 is fixed as a small value and r tends to infinity, and this remains an interesting open case of the problem.'
